metadata.title: Why Your 0.68 Is Really a 0.40
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 05. Source chapter: books/the-reallocation-engine/chapters/05-verifying-the-data.md (the base-rate / confidence-illusion mechanism only). Companion to vox-comma-orphan, which took the join-fragmentation half of the same chapter.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most; 100-square isotype grid as the hero object
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty navy = true sponsor / genuine positive, crimson = false positive / non-sponsor flagged, pale grey = the silent majority of non-sponsors, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty navy #3D5A80 = true sponsor / genuine positive; crimson #BF3339 = false positive; pale grey #D8D2C4 = non-sponsor not flagged; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO Bayes' theorem formula or derivation (the dot grid + sliding marker ARE the argument — no P(A|B) anywhere), NO calibration-curve discussion ('calibrated' is stated once as a property, never charted), NO cost-asymmetry threshold analysis, medical-test framing LIMITED TO ONE BEAT (B05 only), NO verb taxonomy. Verified figures from chapter 5's confidence-illusion section + Fig 5.4: base rate ~8% in this SIC (8 of 100), raw scorer 0.68, corrected posterior ~0.38-0.45 (film says ~0.40, matching the candidate title), overstatement ~50-75%; medical example: 99%-accurate test at 1-in-10,000 prevalence yields ~1 true + ~100 false positives per 10,000, so <1% of alarms are genuine. No exact false-positive tally is stamped on screen (would require inventing a number) — the swamp is shown qualitatively; only the three verified markers 0.08 / 0.68 / 0.40 carry values.

beats.narration_text: A verified score. Properly calibrated. Zero point six-eight — likely. Nothing about the number is wrong. And the real odds are barely zero point four.

card.sub: the base rate the score forgot

beats.narration_text: Here's what your scorer returns for a company. Zero point six-eight. Sourced to real filings, properly calibrated. So you lean in.
beats.scene_description: a printed scorer readout pinned to the newsprint ground — a tidy card reading 'Sponsorship probability: 0.68 — likely sponsor'; slow push-in on the 0.68
beats.image_prompt: printed screenshot of a data tool result card reading 'Sponsorship probability: 0.68 — likely sponsor', clean sans-serif UI, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage

beats.narration_text: But that number answered only half a question. It never asked the other half: how rare is a sponsor here in the first place?

document.quote: How rare is it, before you look?

beats.scene_description: the missing question as a large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'rare' as it is spoken
beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] how rare is it, before you look?

beats.narration_text: In this category, only eight companies in a hundred have ever sponsored. Eight. The other ninety-two never have. That's the base rate.

production_viz.label: 8 in 100 — the base rate
production_viz.mechanic: a 10x10 isotype grid counts up; 8 squares land navy (true sponsors), 92 pale grey (non-sponsors); a serif label under the grid reads '8 sponsors in 100'
production_viz.note: 8% base rate — verbatim from chapter 5 (pharma/medicine-manufacturing SIC)

beats.narration_text: Picture a test that's ninety-nine percent accurate, for a disease one in ten thousand people have. Almost every alarm it raises is a false one.

production_viz.label: 99% accurate, still mostly false alarms
production_viz.mechanic: two stacked bars of alarms: a tall crimson bar (~100 false positives) beside a single navy tick (~1 true positive); serif label 'per 10,000 tested'; the point is the ratio, not the arithmetic
production_viz.note: medical framing — the ONLY beat that uses it (card exclusion). Verified: 99% acc, 1-in-10,000 prevalence, ~1 true : ~100 false, <1% genuine

beats.narration_text: Same shape here. A positive signal fires on our company. But a signal that looks positive lands on rare real sponsors and common non-sponsors alike.
beats.scene_description: the 100-grid returns faint; a 'positive signal' pulse highlights a scattering of squares — a couple navy, but many more grey turning crimson as the signal lands on them too

production_viz.label: the signal lands on both
production_viz.mechanic: faint base-rate grid; a signal ring sweeps; the few navy sponsors light, but many grey squares flip crimson (false positives) because there are simply so many of them
production_viz.note: no exact FP count stamped — qualitative

beats.new_visual_element: [COMPOSITE] the signal fires on sponsors AND non-sponsors

beats.narration_text: And there are so many non-sponsors that the false positives pile up — swamping the handful of real ones underneath them.

production_viz.label: false positives swamp the real ones
production_viz.mechanic: the flagged squares gather into one pile; a few navy at the base, crimson accumulating on top and around them until navy is nearly buried; accumulate move
production_viz.note: the swamp — qualitative; the point is crimson >> navy among the flagged

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] crimson false positives bury the navy few

viz.label: among the flagged, most are false

beats.narration_text: So among everything flagged, the share that's genuine isn't sixty-eight percent. The base rate drags it down, and the marker slides toward forty.
beats.scene_description: a probability line from 0 to 1; a faint anchor at 0.08 (the base rate) tugs left; the 0.68 marker slides left and settles near 0.40; the editor's pen rings the landing point

production_viz.label: 0.68 slides toward 0.40
production_viz.mechanic: horizontal 0-1 axis; small grey tick at 0.08 labelled 'base rate'; navy marker starts at 0.68, slides left to ~0.40; a faint pull-line from 0.08; HandRing lands on the 0.40 landing on 'forty'

annotation.target: the 0.40 landing point on the probability line

beats.narration_text: Nothing was miscalculated. Zero point six-eight is the signal's strength. Zero point four is what it's worth once the rarity is counted.

production_viz.label: signal strength vs. what it's worth
production_viz.mechanic: two big numbers: '0.68' under a navy label 'signal strength', '0.40' under a crimson label 'what it's worth'; an arrow from one to the other labelled 'count the base rate'
production_viz.note: 0.68 = signal, 0.40 = posterior once prior is counted; no formula

viz.label: the two numbers, side by side

beats.narration_text: Which makes the company a lead worth checking, not a promise. A reason to ask directly, before you spend a single day chasing it.
beats.scene_description: the company card returns; two paths fork from it — a navy 'ask directly' arrow and a faded 'apply blindly' arrow struck out; label 'a lead, not a verdict'

production_viz.label: a lead, not a verdict
production_viz.mechanic: a company card; navy arrow 'ask directly' leads on; a faded 'apply blindly' arrow is struck through in crimson; serif label 'a lead, not a verdict'
production_viz.note: the honest read — no cost-ratio threshold math (card exclusion)

beats.new_visual_element: [COMPOSITE] a lead worth checking, not a promise

beats.narration_text: A strong signal for a rare thing is still a long shot.

card.copy: A strong signal for a rare thing is still a long shot.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — a strong signal for a rare thing
